name: Colorectal cancer
presentation: Change in bowel habit (looser/more frequent) >4 weeks, especially in older patients
Rectal bleeding — fresh blood mixed with stool or on paper (right-sided may cause occult bleeding only)
Iron deficiency anaemia — occult blood loss from right-sided tumours
Mass on PR examination or palpable abdominal mass
Positive FIT (faecal immunochemical test) on bowel cancer screening — often asymptomatic at detection
signs: Abdominal mass — palpable tumour, particularly right-sided
Signs of obstruction: distension, tympany, high-pitched bowel sounds
Hepatomegaly or jaundice if liver metastases

bedside: FIT (faecal immunochemical test): screening test; threshold ≥10 μg Hb/g triggers colonoscopy referral
PR examination: mandatory in all suspected colorectal cancer
bloods: CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen): staging and surveillance marker — not diagnostic (low sensitivity); elevated CEA at diagnosis = worse prognosis
FBC: iron deficiency anaemia (microcytic, ↓ferritin) — classic presentation of right-sided colon cancer
LFTs: elevated ALP/bilirubin if liver metastases
imaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: staging for all newly diagnosed CRC — lymph nodes, liver, lung metastases
Colonoscopy: gold standard for diagnosis and biopsy; entire colon must be evaluated
MRI pelvis: mandatory for rectal cancers below peritoneal reflection — assesses mesorectal fascia clearance and guides neoadjuvant therapy

mx: Surgery (only curative option): resection based on tumour location and blood supply — see location table in traps
Rectal cancer: neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy (short-course radiotherapy or long-course CRT) before TME surgery to downstage and reduce local recurrence
Adjuvant/palliative chemotherapy: FOLFOX or FOLFIRI regimens; targeted agents for metastatic disease — bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) or cetuximab (anti-EGFR, RAS wild-type only)
traps: Surgery by location (must memorise): Caecum/ascending/proximal transverse → RIGHT hemicolectomy (ileo-colic anastomosis) | Distal transverse/descending → LEFT hemicolectomy (colo-colic) | Sigmoid → high anterior resection | Upper rectum → anterior resection + TME | Low rectum → low anterior resection + low TME ± defunctioning stoma | Anal verge → abdomino-perineal excision of rectum (APER) — permanent colostomy, no anastomosis
Hartmann's procedure = emergency sigmoid resection + end colostomy (no anastomosis) — used when bowel perforated; colostomy can be reversed later
Extended right hemicolectomy covers caecum to splenic flexure — not appropriate for distal transverse (left hemi is correct); transverse colectomy only for central transverse lesions
MRI mesorectum is mandatory for rectal cancers — determines whether circumferential resection margin is threatened and whether neoadjuvant treatment is needed
CEA is not a screening test — used for post-operative surveillance to detect recurrence; rising CEA post-resection = recurrence until proven otherwise
HNPCC (Lynch syndrome) family: consider panproctocolectomy rather than segmental resection due to high metachronous cancer risk
